turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has our opinion this morning in case 18-15, Kisor v. Wilkie.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: For nearly a century this Court has often deferred to agency's reasonable interpretations of their own ambiguous regulations. We call that practice Auer deference or sometimes Seminole Rock deference, after two decisions in which we used it. The only question presented in this case is whether we should overrule Auer and Seminole Rock and a host of decisions applying them, thus discarding the deference those precedents give to agencies. We decline to do so. The petitioner here is James Kisor, a Vietnam War veteran. In the proceedings below Kisor sought certain disability benefits from the Department of Veterans Affairs, which are called the VA for short. The Board of Veterans' Appeals, which is part of the VA, denied that request based on its interpretation of a particular VA regulation. On appeal, the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit affirmed the Board's decision by invoking Auer deference. In other words, it deferred to the VA's interpretation of the VA's regulation. Kisor now argues, as I said, that we should overrule Auer. Before resolving that question, the Court's opinion clears up some possible uncertainties about what Auer deference is. The doctrine is based on a presumption that Congress would want a Court to defer to the issuing agency's view of an ambiguous rules meaning. We make that presumption for reasons relating to the comparative attributes and competencies of courts and agencies, which often suggest that an agency is the most appropriate interpreter of its own rules. But we also recognize that sometimes the reasons behind that presumption do not apply, or countervailing reasons outweigh them, and when that is so, deference should not be given. So the opinion makes clear both the scope and the limits of Auer deference, where it applies and where it does not. Initially Auer deference can apply only when a regulation is genuinely ambiguous, and by that we mean ambiguous even after a Court has used all its traditional tools of interpretation. If that kind of ambiguity remains, the agency's construction of its rule must still be reasonable, and even some reasonable readings of genuinely ambiguous regulations should not be given deference. That is so particularly when the interpretation is not the official position of the agency, when it is not based in some way on the agency's expertise, or when it does not reflect the agency's fair and considered judgment. In the opinion we describe further what each of those limits means. But outside such circumstances where Auer deference applies, it is real. It is not what courts sometimes call Skidmore deference, which is the supposed deference courts give to things they find persuasive and would adopt any way for themselves. Auer deference means that when there is more than one reasonable interpretation of a genuinely ambiguous regulation, the Court accepts the agencies. And as I said, we rule today that this form of deference should continue. The principle of stare decisis, in English letting decisions stand, is an important one for stability and evenhandedness in the law. To overrule a case we need a special justification and Kisor fails to offer one here. Mainly, he argues that the administrative state has gotten larger and more influential since Seminole Rock and Auer. That may be right as a factual matter and more important we agree that courts must always retain the ability to check agency abuses. That is one reason we have taken care today to reinforce the limits of Auer deference and to emphasize the critical role courts retain in interpreting rules, but it is no answer to the growth of agencies for courts to take over their expertise based functions. Accordingly, as I said before, we decline the invitation to overrule Auer. We still however must remand this case and ask the Federal Circuit to analyze it anew. The Federal Circuit we think did not appropriately consider whether the VA regulation to which it deferred is in fact genuinely ambiguous, and even supposing that regulation is ambiguous, the Court did not consider other reasons why Auer deference might not apply. We therefore vacate the judgment and instruct the Federal Circuit to consider the matter again in light of all the principles outlined in our opinion. The Chief Justice has filed an opinion concurring in part. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment, in which Justice Thomas joins and in which Justice Alito and Justice Kavanaugh join in part. Justice Kavanaugh has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment, in which Justice Alito joins.
